title: Complete rewrite of Producer-Consumer concept content — current grade: F (5/36)
description:
Producer-Consumer has zero hook, zero intuition, zero real-world connection, zero summary. The description 'bounded buffer with producers and consumers, wait/signal sync' is textbook jargon. Step descriptions say WHAT ('producer-1 produces item 3 (buffer: 3/4)') but never WHY. REWRITE:

HOOK: 'Your web server just received 10,000 requests. It can only process 4 at a time. Where do the other 9,996 go? This is the producer-consumer problem — and every message queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS) solves it.'

INTUITION: 'Think of a sushi conveyor belt. The chef (producer) places plates on the belt. Diners (consumers) take plates off. The belt has limited space. If the chef makes sushi faster than diners eat, the belt fills up and the chef must WAIT. If diners eat faster than the chef cooks, they must WAIT for more. The synchronization challenge: how do we coordinate without the chef and diner stepping on each other?'

SAMPLE STEP REWRITES:
- Old: 'producer-1 produces item 3 (buffer: 3/4)'
- New: 'producer-1 adds item 3 to the buffer. The buffer now holds 3 of 4 slots. Because there is still room (3 < 4), the producer can continue without blocking.'
- Old: 'producer-1 waits (buffer full, 4/4)'
- New: 'producer-1 tries to add an item but the buffer is FULL (4/4). It must WAIT until a consumer removes an item and signals that space is available. This is the back-pressure mechanism — without it, the producer would overwrite unconsumed data.'

COMPLEXITY: 'Produce and consume are both O(1) operations. The bottleneck is the synchronization overhead: acquiring/releasing the lock, and potentially blocking when the buffer is full or empty. Buffer size is the key tuning parameter — too small causes frequent blocking, too large wastes memory.'

REAL-WORLD: 'Kafka: producers write to topic partitions (bounded by disk/memory), consumers read at their own pace. RabbitMQ: publishers push to queues, consumers pull. Even your browser uses this: the network thread produces HTTP responses, the main thread consumes them for rendering.'

SUMMARY: '• Producer-Consumer = coordination between data creators and data consumers via shared buffer
• Buffer FULL → producer blocks (back-pressure)
• Buffer EMPTY → consumer blocks (starvation)
• Key: the buffer decouples producer speed from consumer speed'
acceptanceCriteria: Description replaced with hook + intuition (sushi belt analogy)
All step descriptions include WHY the action happens, not just WHAT
Properties panel includes real-world section (Kafka, RabbitMQ, browser)
Memorable 4-bullet summary added at the bottom of properties panel
Teaching score improves from F (5/36) to at least C (18+/36)

title: Complete rewrite of Thread Pool Saturation concept content — current grade: F (5/36)
description:
Thread Pool Saturation has zero hook, zero intuition, zero explanation text, zero real-world context, zero summary. It's just a visualization with step descriptions like 'Tick 3: 4/4 threads busy, queue: 2' and no teaching content at all. REWRITE:

HOOK: 'Every web server you've ever used has a thread pool. When traffic spikes during Black Friday and all threads are busy, what happens to the next 10,000 requests? They queue, then get rejected. This visualizer shows exactly how that cascade works.'

INTUITION: 'Imagine a restaurant with 4 waiters (threads) and 3 chairs in the waiting area (queue). When all waiters are serving and all chairs are taken, the next customer is turned away at the door. That is thread pool saturation — and it is the #1 cause of 503 Service Unavailable errors.'

SAMPLE STEP REWRITES:
- Old: 'Tick 5: SATURATED -- all threads busy, queue full (2 rejected)'
- New: 'SATURATED: All 4 threads are processing tasks and the queue is full (3/3). 2 new tasks have been REJECTED — they will never be processed. In a web server, these would be HTTP 503 errors returned to users.'

REAL-WORLD: 'Java Tomcat default: 200 threads, queue 100. Node.js: single-threaded event loop + Worker pool (4 default). Go: goroutine pool patterns (bounded with semaphore). AWS Lambda: concurrency limit = pool size.'
acceptanceCriteria: Description text added above visualization with hook and intuition
All step descriptions explain consequences (rejected = lost requests)
Real-world examples panel added (Tomcat, Node.js, Go, Lambda)
Summary section with pool sizing formula (Little's Law reference)

title: Complete rewrite of Thread Lifecycle concept content — current grade: F (8/36)
description:
Thread Lifecycle has zero hook, zero intuition, zero real-world context, zero summary. Opens with 'A thread passes through 7 states during its lifetime' — pure definition. Step descriptions are adequate ('start() called — enters ready queue') but no WHY. REWRITE:

HOOK: 'When you call thread.start(), what ACTUALLY happens inside the operating system? The thread doesn't run immediately — it enters a queue, gets scheduled, might get preempted, might wait on a lock. Understanding these 7 states is how you debug 'my thread isn't doing anything' problems.'

INTUITION: 'Think of a thread like a job applicant. NEW = hired but hasn't started yet. RUNNABLE = sitting in the lobby, ready to work. RUNNING = at the desk, doing actual work. BLOCKED = waiting for a meeting room someone else is using. WAITING = told to sit and wait for a notification. TERMINATED = retired.'

SAMPLE STEP REWRITES:
- Old: 'Thread created'
- New: 'Thread created (NEW state). The Thread object exists in memory, but the OS has not allocated any execution resources yet. No CPU time is consumed. The thread is like a signed contract — the employee hasn't shown up for work yet.'

SUMMARY: '• 7 states: New → Runnable → Running → (Blocked|Waiting|Timed-Waiting) → Terminated
• Only the OS scheduler moves threads to RUNNING
• BLOCKED = waiting for lock. WAITING = waiting for signal. Different causes!
• Debug tip: if your thread is stuck in BLOCKED, another thread holds the lock'
acceptanceCriteria: Description replaced with hook about debugging
Job applicant analogy added for each state
All step descriptions include practical WHY and debugging relevance
Summary includes debugging tip for each state

title: Complete rewrite of Readers-Writers concept content — current grade: F (7/36)
description:
Readers-Writers has zero hook, zero intuition, zero summary. Opens with 'The readers-writers problem controls access to a shared resource' — textbook definition. It DOES mention real-world implementations (ReadWriteLock, RWMutex, shared_mutex) which is good but buried. REWRITE:

HOOK: 'A shared Google Doc lets 50 people read at the same time, but only one person can edit at a time. That is the readers-writers problem — and every database uses it (READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE are all variations).'

INTUITION: 'Imagine a museum. Visitors (readers) can wander freely — they only look, so multiple visitors are fine. But when the curator (writer) needs to rearrange exhibits, ALL visitors must leave first, and no new visitors enter until the curator finishes. The question is: who gets priority? If visitors keep arriving, the curator never gets a turn (writer starvation).'

SAMPLE STEP REWRITES:
- Old: 'reader-1 starts reading (active readers: 2)'
- New: 'reader-1 starts reading. There are now 2 concurrent readers. This is safe because readers only OBSERVE — they never modify the data. Multiple readers can coexist without corruption. (This is why database read replicas can serve unlimited read traffic.)'

SUMMARY: '• Readers = concurrent (safe, read-only). Writers = exclusive (must be alone).
• Reader-preference → writers starve. Writer-preference → readers starve.
• Fair policy → FIFO ordering, no starvation, but lower throughput.
• Real-world: database read replicas, file system shared locks, Java ReadWriteLock'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook references Google Docs and database isolation levels
Museum analogy for readers (visitors) vs writers (curator)
Step descriptions explain WHY concurrent reads are safe
Starvation explicitly visualized and explained

title: Rewrite Race Condition concept content — current grade: F (11/36)
description:
Race Condition has decent visuals (split panel, histogram) but weak teaching text. Opens with 'A race condition occurs when multiple threads access shared data without synchronization' — definition first. Steps say WHAT not WHY for most events. REWRITE:

HOOK: 'Two ATMs. One bank account. $100 balance. Both withdraw $80 at the same time. Final balance: $20 or -$60? It depends on which ATM's write hits the database last. This is a race condition — and it is the #1 most common concurrency bug in production systems.'

INTUITION: 'The problem is the gap between READ and WRITE. Thread A reads $100. Thread B reads $100 (same stale value!). Thread A writes $20. Thread B writes $20 (not -$60, because it read $100 too!). One withdrawal is lost. The fix: make read-modify-write ATOMIC — lock the account while one thread is working on it.'

SAMPLE STEP REWRITES:
- Old: 'thread-0 reads counter = 0'
- New: 'thread-0 reads counter = 0. This value is now in thread-0's LOCAL register. If another thread changes the counter before thread-0 writes back, thread-0 won't know — it will write based on this STALE value.'

SUMMARY: '• Race = two threads access same data, at least one writes, no synchronization
• Root cause: stale reads between read and write
• Fix: make read-modify-write atomic (mutex, CAS, or atomic variable)
• Test: run 100 times — non-deterministic bugs appear as value distributions'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook uses ATM/bank analogy
Intuition explains the read-write gap explicitly
All step descriptions mention stale value risk
Histogram section explains WHY running many times reveals the bug

title: Rewrite Dining Philosophers concept content — current grade: D (13/36)
description:
Dining Philosophers has the classic analogy built-in (philosophers at table) but weak hook, no real-world connection, no mistakes section, no summary. REWRITE:

HOOK: 'This problem was invented by Dijkstra in 1965 for an exam question. It has been the standard way to teach deadlock for 60 years because it makes an invisible problem VISIBLE — you can literally see the circular dependency.'

ADD COMMON MISTAKES: '• Mistake: Adding more forks. Wrong! The issue is circular waiting, not resource scarcity.
• Mistake: Making philosophers take turns. This works but eliminates parallelism.
• Mistake: Using a random delay. This only makes deadlock less likely, not impossible.'

ADD REAL-WORLD: 'Database transactions: Transaction A locks row 1, needs row 2. Transaction B locks row 2, needs row 1. Deadlock! MySQL resolves this by detecting the cycle and aborting one transaction (the victim).'

SUMMARY: '• Deadlock requires ALL 4 conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
• Resource ordering breaks circular wait (the simplest production fix)
• Detection+rollback is used when prevention is too expensive (databases)'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook mentions Dijkstra's exam and 60-year history
Common mistakes section added with 3 specific errors
Database deadlock real-world example added
Summary with 3-bullet takeaway including Coffman conditions

title: Rewrite Sleeping Barber concept content — current grade: D (12/36)
description:
Sleeping Barber has good inherent analogy (barber shop IS the intuition) and mentions real-world connections (connection pools, thread pools) but no hook explaining WHY this matters and no step-level WHY. REWRITE:

HOOK: 'Every connection pool you've ever used (database connections, HTTP clients, gRPC channels) is a sleeping barber. The pool sleeps when idle, wakes on request, queues when busy, and rejects when full. Understanding this problem means understanding every pool in your production stack.'

SAMPLE STEP REWRITES:
- Old: 'customer-3 arrives, wakes the barber'
- New: 'customer-3 arrives and finds the barber sleeping (no active requests). It WAKES the barber by signaling the semaphore. The race condition risk: what if the barber checks for customers at the exact moment a customer checks the barber's state? A mutex protects this check-then-act sequence.'

SUMMARY: '• Sleeping Barber = server that sleeps when idle, wakes on demand
• Waiting room capacity = bounded queue size
• Customer leaves when full = request rejection (HTTP 503)
• Core challenge: avoid race between barber-checks-empty and customer-checks-sleeping'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook connects to connection pools explicitly
Step descriptions explain the synchronization risk at each state transition
Summary maps barber concepts to production equivalents

title: Rewrite Deadlock Demo concept content — current grade: D (15/36)
description:
Deadlock Demo has excellent visualization (arrow graph, pulsing deadlock) and mentions Coffman conditions but weak hook and zero real-world examples. REWRITE:

HOOK: 'In 2017, a deadlock in Amazon's S3 metadata service took down half the internet for 4 hours. Deadlocks are not academic curiosities — they are production outages. This demo shows exactly how circular wait forms and how one simple rule prevents it.'

ADD REAL-WORLD: '• MySQL: InnoDB deadlock detector runs every ~1 second, picks a "victim" transaction to roll back
• Go: runtime detects "all goroutines are asleep" and panics
• Java: jstack shows thread dump with deadlock detection
• Production fix: lock ordering (sort locks by ID) + timeout + retry'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook references a real production incident (S3 2017 or similar)
Properties panel adds MySQL/Go/Java deadlock detection examples
Coffman conditions are emphasized as the framework, not just mentioned
Each condition is visually toggled in the demo so students see which one breaks the deadlock

title: Upgrade Event Loop concept content — current grade: C (23/36) → target B
description:
Event Loop is the best-taught concept (grade C, 23/36) but can reach B. Missing: explicit common mistakes, deeper real-world context, and a self-test prompt. ADD:

COMMON MISTAKES: '• Thinking setTimeout(0) runs immediately — it still goes through the macrotask queue after all microtasks drain
• Thinking async/await makes code parallel — it's still single-threaded, just non-blocking
• Forgetting that Promise.then() callbacks are microtasks, not macrotasks'

REAL-WORLD: 'Node.js servers are entirely event-loop driven. When your Express handler calls await db.query(), the thread is NOT blocked — it's freed to handle other requests. This is why Node.js can handle 10K concurrent connections on a single thread.'

SELF-TEST: Before each demo, add: 'PREDICT: What will be printed? Try to figure out the order before clicking Step.'
acceptanceCriteria: Common mistakes section added with 3 specific JS concurrency errors
Node.js real-world example explains why single-threaded is actually efficient
Each demo adds 'Predict the output order before stepping through' prompt
Teaching score reaches 27+ (B grade)

title: Upgrade Go Goroutines concept content — current grade: C (20/36) → target B
description:
Go Goroutines has the best edge case coverage (5 demos, goroutine leak shown) and excellent visuals but missing hook, real-world context, and explicit common mistakes. ADD:

HOOK: 'Go handles millions of concurrent connections at Uber, Cloudflare, and Docker. Its secret: goroutines cost ~2KB of stack each (vs ~1MB for OS threads). You can spawn 1 million goroutines on a laptop. This demo shows how they communicate.'

COMMON MISTAKES: '• Goroutine leak: spawning a goroutine that blocks on a channel nobody reads from (our Select demo shows this!)
• Closing a channel from the receiver side (only senders should close)
• Not using context.WithCancel for cancellation (bare goroutines can't be stopped)'

REAL-WORLD: 'Docker: goroutines per container for health checks. Kubernetes: goroutines per watch stream. CockroachDB: goroutines for distributed transaction coordination.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook cites Uber/Cloudflare/Docker with goroutine cost comparison
Common mistakes section highlights goroutine leaks (linking to the select demo)
Real-world section names 3 production Go systems
Teaching score reaches 26+ (B grade)

title: Upgrade Async Patterns concept content — current grade: C (19/36) → target B
description:
Async Patterns has the BEST step descriptions in the module (grade 3/3 on T4) and excellent edge case coverage (6 demos) but weak hook and zero mistakes/real-world. ADD:

HOOK: 'Every React component that fetches data uses one of these 4 combinators. Choose wrong and your app either crashes on the first error (Promise.all), ignores errors silently (Promise.allSettled), or races to a random result (Promise.race). This demo shows exactly when to use each one.'

COMMON MISTAKES: '• Using Promise.all when you want partial results — one failure kills everything. Use allSettled instead.
• Using Promise.race for fallback — it settles on first rejection too. Use Promise.any for first SUCCESS.
• Forgetting that rejected promises in Promise.all still execute to completion — they just don't contribute to the result.'

REAL-WORLD: 'React Query: uses Promise.all for parallel queries. Cloudflare Workers: Promise.race with timeout for latency hedging. Next.js: Promise.allSettled for ISR revalidation.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook connects to React data fetching decisions
Common mistakes contrasts all vs allSettled and race vs any
Real-world examples cite React Query, Cloudflare, Next.js

title: Upgrade Lock Comparison concept content — current grade: C (22/36) → target B
description:
Lock Comparison has the best summary (Rule of Thumb box) and best comparison (3-way) in the module but missing real-world context and explicit common mistakes. ADD:

HOOK: 'The Linux kernel uses spinlocks for interrupt handlers (can't sleep in interrupt context!). Java's synchronized keyword is a blocking mutex. Go's sync.Mutex is a hybrid (spins briefly, then sleeps). Choosing wrong means either wasting CPU or adding latency. This demo lets you see the difference.'

COMMON MISTAKES: '• Using a spinlock for I/O operations — the thread burns 100% CPU while waiting for a disk read that takes milliseconds
• Using a blocking mutex for a 3-instruction critical section — the context switch costs more than the work
• Ignoring TTAS — most production spinlocks use test-and-test-and-set, not naive test-and-set'

REAL-WORLD: 'Linux kernel: spinlock_t for interrupt handlers. Java: ReentrantLock (blocking mutex with fairness option). Go: sync.Mutex (adaptive — spins then sleeps). Rust: parking_lot::Mutex (hybrid, outperforms std).'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook cites Linux kernel, Java synchronized, Go sync.Mutex
Common mistakes contrasts spinlock+I/O (bad) vs mutex+short-CS (bad)
Real-world section names kernel, Java, Go, Rust lock implementations

title: Add module-wide content style guide enforcing hook-intuition-why-summary pattern
description:
The module has no content consistency. Best concepts (Event Loop, Lock Comparison) have hooks and summaries. Worst (Producer-Consumer, Thread Pool) have none. Create a content style guide that ALL concepts must follow:

1. HOOK (2-3 sentences): Real-world problem or surprising fact. Never start with a definition.
2. INTUITION (2-3 sentences): Physical analogy before any technical language.
3. STEP DESCRIPTIONS: Every step must include WHY, connecting to the algorithm's strategy.
4. COMMON MISTAKES (2-3 bullets): What students actually get wrong (search Reddit).
5. REAL-WORLD (2-3 bullets): Where this is used in production, with specific system names.
6. SUMMARY (3-4 bullets): Flashcard-style takeaway.

Document this as a comment block at the top of ConcurrencyModule.tsx (after splitting) or in a CONTENT_GUIDELINES.md file.
acceptanceCriteria: Style guide document created with the 6-section template
Guide includes one worked example (e.g., Race Condition fully written out)
All future concept additions must follow this template

title: Add self-test prediction prompts to all 12 concepts
description:
T11 (Self-Test) averages 0.42/3 — the second-weakest dimension. Only Event Loop naturally invites prediction. For EVERY concept, add a 'PREDICT' prompt before the first step plays:

- Race Condition: 'If 3 threads each increment a counter 3 times without locks, what will the final value be? (Hint: it won't be 9)'
- Producer-Consumer: 'With 2 producers and buffer size 4, how many items can be produced before the first producer blocks?'
- Dining Philosophers: 'All 5 philosophers pick up their left fork simultaneously. What happens next?'
- Event Loop: 'What order will these console.logs print? Write your answer before stepping through.'
- Thread Lifecycle: 'Thread A calls start(). Does it run immediately?'
- Goroutines: 'What happens when you send to an unbuffered channel with no receiver?'
- And so on for all 12.

Display as a dismissible callout above the canvas that disappears after the user starts stepping.
acceptanceCriteria: All 12 concepts have a PREDICT prompt visible before first step
Prompts are specific to the demo content (not generic)
Prompts disappear after first Step/Play interaction
At least 3 prompts are counter-intuitive (the answer surprises students)

title: Add complexity/performance intuition text to all concepts missing it
description:
T5 (Complexity Intuition) averages 0.33/3 — the weakest dimension. Only Lock Comparison and Event Loop score above 0. For each concept, add intuitive performance explanations:

- Race Condition: 'Mutex overhead is ~25ns uncontended on modern x86. With contention, it can be 1000x slower (25μs). This is why lock-free alternatives exist for hot paths.'
- Producer-Consumer: 'Buffer size is the key tuning parameter. Too small → producers block frequently. Too large → memory waste. Kafka default: 50MB per partition.'
- Dining Philosophers: 'With N philosophers, the ordered solution's throughput is N/2 (every other philosopher eats simultaneously). The naive solution's throughput is 0 after deadlock.'
- Thread Lifecycle: 'Context switch cost: ~1-10μs on Linux. If your critical section is shorter than that, use a spinlock instead of a blocking mutex.'

Do NOT just show O(n) notation. Explain what it MEANS in real terms (nanoseconds, throughput, memory).
acceptanceCriteria: At least 8 of 12 concepts have performance intuition text
No bare O(n) notation without explanation of what it means in practice
At least 3 concepts include specific numbers (nanoseconds, throughput)
